thread.title: Sen. Angus King introduces bill to preserve and manage public lands | Local News | foxbangor.com
thread.title_full: Sen. Angus King introduces bill to preserve and manage public lands | Local News | foxbangor.com

author: David Ledford
title: Sen. Angus King introduces bill to preserve and manage public lands | Local News | foxbangor.com
text:
WASHINGTON, D.C. -- A new bill recently introduced by Senator Angus King could have a big impact in Acadia National Park -- it would increase funding to continue fixing aging infrastructure in Acadia and National Parks across the country.
"America's greatest idea -- national parks -- needs investment," said Eric Stiles, president and CEO of Friends of Acadia.
There's a new effort in the works to repair and maintain our national parks.
The America the Beautiful Act is a new bipartisan bill introduced by Senator Angus King (I-ME) and 3 co-sponsors from other states: Sen. Steve Daines (R-MT), Sen. Kevin Cramer (R-SD) and Sen. Mark Warner (D-VA) to preserve and manage the country's public lands.
"When you go to a national park, you need roads, you need parking areas, you need restrooms, maintenance buildings, the carriage roads, the trails," said Stiles.
The act would reauthorize the National Parks and Public Land Legacy Restoration Fund originally passed in 2020 as part of the Great American Outdoors Act .
The bill would also increase LRF funding to help address growing maintenance needs in national parks.
In a statement, Senator King said quote:
"People travel from every corner of the world to connect with America’s incredible natural beauty and to make memories that last a lifetime,” said Senator King. "However, deferred repairs on aging infrastructure like roads and trails can become unsafe for visitors and diminish the quality of our public lands and National Parks.
The bipartisan America the Beautiful Act protects our lands and the visitor experience — by addressing maintenance backlogs and reauthorizing the historic Legacy Restoration Fund.
This legislation is an important step forward in establishing lasting protections for our public lands, and serves as a proud reminder that stewardship is not a partisan issue."
In Acadia National Park, Stiles says maintenance could include repairs to the Jordan Pond House and improvements in well-used areas of the park.
He also says funding national parks is essential because of the economic impact they have on the states they're in.
"Here at Acadia National Park we get about 4 million visits a year. The total economic impact in the private sector in Downeast Maine alone is over $650 million -- that's big business," said Stiles.
Stiles says funding park maintenance now can help to preserve the land for future generations.
"Imagine you bought a home but you didn't have money to repair your roof. The first leak causes a little bit of damage, but 10 years later the cost keeps increasing and increasing," said Stiles.
To read the full text of the bill, click here .
According to a release from Sen. King -- in addition to reauthorizing and strengthening the LRF through 2033, the America the Beautiful Act would:
Increase funding from $1.9 billion to $2 billion per year Prioritize projects that have a 15 percent match from stakeholders Require agencies to have a two-year running list of projects Allow the agencies to move forward with next year’s projects under a Continuing Resolution Ensure that all lands administered by the Fish and Wildlife Service are eligible Bolster the ability for public donations to the fund by requiring agencies to run public awareness campaigns and make known the ability to donate Require the agencies to assess and dispose of unneeded constructed assets Require additional reporting to Congress on actions taken by the agency to reduce their Deferred Maintenance backlogs Facebook Twitter WhatsApp SMS Email Print Copy article link Save David Ledford Reporter
